memoryArtifacts.headline: BIO
memoryArtifacts.description:
BIO:  from Collin's Peerage of England, Vol. VII, p. 110

The ancestors of the Leigh family assumed their name for the town of High-Leigh in Cheshire, where they were seated before the Norman invasion …

nameMeanings.name: de
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Indian Dilip, Anirban, Gopal, Alok, Anindya, Arunava, Asit, Asok, Dibyendu, Goutam, Indra, Indranil.

variants.name: Dey
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Indian Ashit, Indu, Naina, Partha, Soumya, Ajit, Anjan, Anjana, Anup, Arjun, Arun, Ashim.
English, Scottish, and Welsh: variant of   Day  .
German: topographic name from a field name in North Rhine-Westphalia, denoting a sizeable piece of land, from Middle Low German dē ‘thigh’ (referring to the shape of the property).

nameMeanings.name: Legh
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of Family Names in Britain and Ireland © University of the West of England 2016

variants.name: Leigh
meanings: English: habitational name from any of numerous places (in at least sixteen counties, but especially Leigh in Lancashire) named either with the nominative case of Old English lēah ‘woodland clearing’ (see Lee ) or with lēage, a late dative form of this word (see Lye ).
Chinese: variant Romanization of the surname 李, based on its Cantonese pronunciation. See   Li   1.

nameMeanings.name: William
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Probably the most successful of all the Old French names of Germanic origin that were introduced to England by the Normans. It is derived from Germanic wil ‘will, desire’ + helm ‘helmet, protection’. The fact that it was borne by the Conqueror himself does not seem to have inhibited its favour with the ‘conquered’ population: in the first century after the Conquest it was the commonest male name of all, and not only among the Normans. In the later Middle Ages it was overtaken by John , but continued to run second to that name until the 20th century, when the picture became more fragmented.

historicEvents.title: The Battle of Tichebray
historicEvents.description: King Henry I defeated and captured his brother, Robert of Normandy, at the Battle of Tinchebray in 1106. Robert remained in captivity until his death in 1134.

historicEvents.title: Magna Carta
historicEvents.description: King John of England agreed to a charter of rights called the Magna Carta. The document was originally drafted by the Archbishop of Canterbury to establish peace between King John and a group of rebel barons. The Magna Carta was completed in 1215 and is now considered to be one of the greatest constitutional documents of all time.

en.lifeSummary: William de Legh was born in High Legh, Cheshire, England as the son of Sir Hamon de la Legh Crompton and Agnes de Bohun. He married Lady Margery Oughtrington in High Legh, Cheshire, England, United Kingdom. They were the parents of at least 2 sons. He died about 1220, in Northwich, Cheshire, England.

ko.West Hall, High Leigh, Cheshire, England: 영국 잉글랜드 데폰 히리 스트 메리
ko.lifeSummary: William de Legh(은)는 영국 체실르 히 레그h에서 Sir Hamon de la Legh Crompton(와)과 Agnes de Bohun의 아들로 태어났다. 그는 영국 잉글랜드 체실르 히 레그h에서 Lady Margery Oughtrington(와)과 결혼했다. 그들은 적어도 두 명의 아들을 두었다. 그는 대략 1220년 영국 체실르 노르스위치에서 사망했다.
